The club has since oscillated between Serie A and B, a shadow of its 90s peak but still carrying the most cosmopolitan trophy cabinet of any Italian provincial club.
rivals: Bologna — the Emilia-Romagna regional derby, the two clubs' closest geographic and cultural neighbour
Juventus — 1990s resentment over Juventus buying Parma's best players (Buffon, Cannavaro)
Reggiana — the lower Emilia regional derby
watch: The 1990s Gialloblu side is one of the most beloved and most dissipated great teams in European football — if you like the tragic arc of a club that had everything and lost it, Parma is your story.
lastSeason: Finished 13th (45 points), surviving comfortably in the top flight.

name: Lecce
honours: No major trophies; multiple Serie B titles
identity: The southernmost club in Serie A, from the heel of Italy's boot — Lecce represents Salento and the deep south, a region with little football history and a fierce local pride that makes the club a cultural flagship.
history: Lecce spent most of its first century bouncing between Serie B and C, with intermittent Serie A spells in the 1980s and 1990s that rarely lasted more than a season or two. The club's identity is tied to the Salento region — the deep south, geographically and economically peripheral to Italian football's northern concentration. Under managers like Fabio Liverani and more recently Roberto D'Aversa, Lecce has become a more stable Serie A presence in the 2020s, trading on a passionate local support base and a reputation for attacking, expressive football that suits the region's baroque, theatrical culture.
rivals: Bari — the Derby del Sud-Est, the great Puglia regional rivalry, intensified when both are in the same division
Foggia — historical Apulian rivalry from the lower divisions
Salernitana — the deep-south solidarity, occasionally hostile
watch: If you want to follow a club that embodies a specific place — Salento, its dialect, its food, its distance from the wealthy north — Lecce is the most geographically committed choice in Serie A.
lastSeason: Finished 17th (38 points), securing another season of top-flight survival on the final stretch.

name: Venezia
honours: 1 Coppa Italia (1941), multiple Serie B titles
identity: Football in a sinking city — Venezia plays in the oldest stadium in Italy, on the edge of a lagoon, and carries a melancholic, faded-grandeur identity unlike any other club in world football.
history: Venezia's Penzo stadium, opened in 1913, is Italy's oldest football ground, and the club's history is inseparable from the city's broader decline as a political and commercial capital. A single Coppa Italia in 1941 is the club's only major honour. Venezia spent decades in the lower divisions, with occasional Serie A spells in the 1990s and 2000s, before a 2021 promotion under Paolo Zanetti introduced a stylish possession identity that briefly caught attention. The 2024 promotion under Eusebio Di Francesco returned Venezia to Serie A, but financial fragility and the stadium's ageing infrastructure mean the club's identity is built more on its setting than its results.
rivals: Hellas Verona — the Veneto regional rivalry, the two historic regional capitals
Padova — the local Veneto derby, dormant at the top level
Genoa — the two oldest maritime republics, occasional tension
watch: No club in the world offers a more singular setting — a stadium on a lagoon edge in a sinking medieval city — and the contrast between the romantic location and the gritty football reality is the whole point.
lastSeason: Spent 2025-26 in Serie B after relegation from Serie A at the end of 2024-25.

name: VfB Stuttgart
honours: 5 German league titles, 3 DFB-Pokal, 1 Bundesliga promotion record
identity: The flagship club of southern Germany's industrial heartland, defined by a yo-yo decade between decline and revival — Stuttgart's 2024-25 season under Sebastian Hoeneß produced one of the most improbable title races in Bundesliga history.
history: Stuttgart was one of the Bundesliga's founding members and won five titles across the 1950s to 2000s, including the 2007 championship under Armin Veh that briefly restored the club to Germany's top table. The 2010s saw a slow decline into a yo-yo pattern, with relegations in 2016 and 2019 and a general sense of drift beneath Bayern's Bavarian dominance. The 2023 appointment of Sebastian Hoeneß — a member of the Hoeneß football dynasty — transformed the club, and Stuttgart briefly led the 2024-25 Bundesliga title race deep into the season with a young, high-pressing side, an extraordinary revival for a club that had been in the second division three years earlier.
rivals: Bayern Munich — the southern-Germany regional rivalry, the two biggest Bavarian-Swabian clubs
1899 Hoffenheim — the Baden-Württemberg regional rivalry, sharpened by Hoffenheim's modern money
Karlsruher SC — the historic Baden-Württemberg derby
watch: The Hoeneß-led revival — from relegation candidates to genuine title contenders in two seasons — is one of the most dramatic recent rebuilds in German football and a study in how quickly a club's trajectory can change under the right coach.
lastSeason: Finished 4th (62 points) to reach the Champions League, driven by Deniz Undav's 19 goals.
keyPlayers: Deniz Undav — 19 goals, powered the Champions League push

name: SC Freiburg
honours: No major trophies; 2 Bundesliga promotions, regular European qualification
identity: Germany's most respected sustainable club — a small Black Forest city side that has consistently overachieved through one of the country's most productive academies and the long, patient stewardship of coach Christian Streich.
history: Freiburg spent most of its first century in regional football before reaching the Bundesliga in the 1990s under Volker Finke, the coach who first established the club's development-first identity. The Christian Streich era (2011-2024) became the longest managerial tenure in German professional football and defined the club: a modest budget, a relentless academy output, a willingness to sell and rebuild, and two German Cup finals (2022 and 2024) that the club lost but treated as validation of its model. The move to the new Europa-Park Stadion in 2021 stabilised revenue and allowed the club to qualify for European competition.
rivals: VfB Stuttgart — the Baden-Württemberg regional rivalry
1899 Hoffenheim — the regional rivalry with a club seen as Freiburg's financial opposite
Karlsruher SC — the historic Baden derby
watch: If you want a club that does things the right way and has the patience to prove it, Freiburg is Germany's case study in sustainability over spending — the Streich era is the model.
lastSeason: Finished 7th (47 points), qualifying for the Conference League — another overachieving season on a modest budget.

name: 1. FSV Mainz 05
honours: No major trophies; sustained Bundesliga presence since 2009
identity: A modest Rhineland club that punched above its weight as the incubator of Jürgen Klopp and Thomas Tuchel — Mainz is German football's quiet coaching laboratory, a place that develops managers as much as players.
history: Mainz spent most of its first century in regional and second-division football before reaching the Bundesliga in 2004 under Jürgen Klopp, whose high-pressing, emotional style was first formed here. The club's identity as a coach-development hub continued with Thomas Tuchel (2009-2014), whose Mainz side briefly qualified for European competition and established the club as a Bundesliga mainstay. Since then, Mainz has remained in the top flight through a succession of young, tactically-inclined coaches, surviving relegation scrapes more often than challenging for European places, but maintaining a respected reputation for developing talent and ideas.
rivals: Eintracht Frankfurt — the Rhine-Main regional rivalry, the two cities' close geographic proximity
Darmstadt 98 — the smaller Rhine-Main regional rivalry
1. FC Kaiserslautern — the historic Rhineland-Palatinate rivalry
watch: If you're interested in clubs that produce coaches rather than trophies — Klopp and Tuchel both cut their teeth here — Mainz is the Bundesliga's coaching academy and a study in how a small club builds a lasting identity through ideas.
lastSeason: Finished 10th (40 points) following a mid-season managerial change that brought in Urs Fischer.

name: FC Augsburg
honours: No major trophies; Bundesliga presence since 2011, 1 European qualification (2015)
identity: A modest Bavarian club that emerged from the shadow of Munich to establish itself as a stable Bundesliga presence — Augsburg is the unglamorous overachiever of southern German football.
history: Augsburg spent most of its first century in regional football before a 2011 promotion to the Bundesliga, the club's first ever top-flight season, achieved under Jos Luhukay. The 2014-15 season under Markus Weinzierl produced a remarkable fifth-place finish and the club's first ever European qualification. Since then, Augsburg has become one of the Bundesliga's most consistent mid-table presences, surviving through pragmatic football, shrewd scouting, and the financial stability of its ownership. The club's relationship with Bavarian giants Bayern Munich is one of geographic proximity and permanent inferiority rather than competitive tension.
rivals: 1860 Munich — the historic Bavarian regional rivalry from the lower divisions
FC Ingolstadt 04 — the recent Bavarian rivalry, dormant as Ingolstadt declined
Nürnberg — the traditional Bavarian regional derby
watch: Augsburg is the Bundesliga club for fans who want to follow a team that does the boring things well and survives — the club's consistency in a wealthy league on a modest budget is the story.
lastSeason: Finished 9th (43 points), a solid mid-table campaign.

name: Werder Bremen
honours: 4 Bundesliga titles, 6 DFB-Pokal, 1 European Cup Winners' Cup (1992)
identity: Northern Germany's great romantic club — a riverside stadium, a green-and-white identity, and a 2000s peak under Thomas Schaaf that briefly made Werder the most attractive team in the country.
history: Werder Bremen's golden era under Thomas Schaaf (1999-2013) produced four Bundesliga titles and six DFB-Pokal wins, with the 2003-04 double built around Johan Micoud, Ailton, and Mladen Klasnic standing as one of the great attacking sides of Bundesliga history. The 1992 European Cup Winners' Cup added a continental honour. After Schaaf's departure, the club declined into a yo-yo pattern, including a 2021 relegation that ended a 40-year Bundesliga stay. Promotion back in 2022 restored top-flight status, and the club has since stabilised, though the 2000s peak feels increasingly distant.
rivals: Hamburger SV — the Nordderby, the great northern-Germany rivalry, dormant as Hamburg declined
Bayern Munich — historical 2000s title rivalry
VfL Wolfsburg — the northern regional rivalry
watch: The 2003-04 double side is one of the Bundesliga's most beloved attacking teams — if you want to understand why German football fell in love with a small northern club, start with that season.
lastSeason: Finished 15th (32 points), surviving after a mid-season managerial change to Daniel Thioune.

name: 1899 Hoffenheim
honours: No major trophies; Bundesliga presence since 2008, 1 Champions League group stage (2018-19)
identity: The village club that money built — Hoffenheim was a seventh-tier amateur side until SAP co-founder Dietmar Hopp's millions turned it into a Bundesliga team in a town of 36,000, making it German football's most controversial modern project.
history: Founded in 1899, Hoffenheim spent more than a century as an obscure regional club from the village of Sinsheim in Baden-Württemberg. The 2000 arrival of Dietmar Hopp — a local boy turned SAP billionaire — transformed the club through sustained investment that took Hoffenheim from the seventh tier to the Bundesliga in eight years. The 2008-09 half-season under Ralf Rangnick, with a young squad built around Vedad Ibisevic and Demba Ba, briefly led the Bundesliga before injuries derailed the campaign. The club has remained in the top flight since, with occasional European runs, but its corporate-backed, village-scale identity sits awkwardly against German football's fan-ownership traditions and has made Hoffenheim one of the league's most resented clubs among traditional supporters.
rivals: VfB Stuttgart — the Baden-Württemberg regional rivalry, sharpened by Hoffenheim's modern money challenging the traditional order
SC Freiburg — the regional rivalry, the money vs sustainability contrast
Eintracht Frankfurt — a broader tension with clubs that resent Hoffenheim's model
watch: Hoffenheim is the Bundesliga's case study in what money can and can't build — a Champions League team in a village of 36,000, and one of the most discussed experiments in modern German football.
lastSeason: Finished 5th (61 points) — a standout campaign — to qualify for the Europa League.

name: OGC Nice
honours: 4 French league titles, 3 Coupe de France
identity: A Riviera club with a proud mid-century pedigree, owned since 2016 by INEOS (Jim Ratcliffe), that blends a Mediterranean setting with growing ambition to disrupt France's traditional hierarchy.
history: Founded in 1904, Nice won four French league titles across the 1950s and 1960s, the club's golden era, before decades of inconsistency and a relegation in the 1990s. The opening of the Allianz Riviera in 2013 and INEOS's 2016 takeover revived ambition, bringing European football and a modern recruitment model to the Côte d'Azur.
rivals: Monaco — the Derby de la Côte d'Azur, France's most scenic regional rivalry
Marseille — a broader south-of-France antipathy
watch: A club backed by one of world football's wealthiest owners that still operates beneath PSG's shadow — a good pick for fans who want ambition without the baggage of dominance.
lastSeason: Finished 16th (32 points) and survived via the relegation play-off, beating Saint-Étienne 4-1 on aggregate, after a mid-season managerial change.

name: Stade Rennais FC
nick: Les Rouges et Noirs (The Red and Blacks)
identity: A Brittany-rooted club whose identity is inseparable from its region — one of France's most historically distinctive provinces — and one of the country's most productive academies.
history: Founded in 1901, Rennes spent decades as a respected but trophy-light provincial club, reaching the Coupe de France final multiple times before finally winning it in 1965 and 1971. The François Pinault era (from 1998) funded a modern academy that produced a stream of French internationals, and the club won its third and most recent Coupe de France in 2019.
rivals: Nantes — the Derby Breton, one of France's oldest regional rivalries
Lorient — a more recent Brittany derby
watch: One of France's premier talent factories — a club where you can reliably watch the next wave of French internationals emerge before they move on.
lastSeason: Finished 6th (59 points), qualifying for the Europa League; Esteban Lepaul (who played for both Angers and Rennes across the season) led Ligue 1 with 21 goals.
keyPlayers: Esteban Lepaul — 2025-26 Ligue 1 top scorer with 21 goals

name: RC Strasbourg Alsace
honours: 1 French league title (1979), 3 Coupe de France
identity: Alsace's only top-flight club and a symbol of a border region whose identity has oscillated between France and Germany — a club whose existence is inseparable from regional pride and European history.
history: Founded in 1906, Strasbourg won its only French league title in 1979 under the management of Gilbert Gress, a local hero. The club has endured multiple relegations and near-bankruptcies, including a 2011 liquidation, before a remarkable comeback from the fifth tier culminating in a 2017 return to Ligue 1 and a 2019 Coupe de la Ligue triumph. Ownership passed to Todd Boehly's group in 2023.
rivals: Metz — the Derby de l'Est, a cross-border regional rivalry
Lens — a broader northern working-class rivalry
watch: A club whose history mirrors Alsace's own: borderlands, identity crises, and stubborn survival — a pick for fans who want a genuinely regional story.
lastSeason: Finished 8th (53 points), a strong season for the young, data-driven BlueCo-owned project.

name: Racing Club de Lens
nick: Les Sang et Or (Blood and Gold)
honours: 1 French league title (1998), 2 Coupe de la Ligue
identity: A mining-town club whose 'Blood and Gold' colors are inseparable from the Pas-de-Calais coalfields — the most intensely community-rooted club in French football, with a stadium that holds nearly the town's entire population.
history: Founded in 1906 in the coal-mining town of Lens, the club's identity was shaped by the working-class immigrant communities that worked the pits — Polish, Italian, and North African. Lens won its only league title in 1998 under Daniel Leclercq, and the Stade Bollaert-Delelis (capacity ~38,000 in a town of ~35,000) is one of Europe's most atmospherically intense grounds. The club returned to Ligue 1 in 2020 and challenged for the title in 2022–23.
rivals: Lille — the Derby du Nord, a historic northern France rivalry
Paris Saint-Germain — a more recent northern-vs-capital rivalry
watch: If you want the loudest, most community-rooted matchday experience in France — a club where the stadium literally embodies the town — this is the pick.
lastSeason: Finished 2nd (70 points) AND won the Coupe de France under manager Pierre Sage, earning Champions League football; three Lens players made the Team of the Season.
keyPlayers: Florian Thauvin — Team of the Season, experienced attacking leader
Pierre Sage — manager who drove the 2nd-place-and-cup double

name: Stade Brestois 29
honours: No major national trophies; first sustained Ligue 1 era began 2019
identity: A Brittany coastal club with a swashbuckling 'Pirates' nickname, a small stadium, and one of French football's most improbable recent overachievement stories.
history: Founded in 1950, Brest spent most of its history in the lower divisions, with brief Ligue 1 stints in the 1980s and 2010s that ended in relegation. A 2019 promotion under Eric Roy's later management became something far more: a club that not only survived but finished in European places by 2024, qualifying for the Champions League against all odds and defying its modest budget.
rivals: Nantes — a Brittany regional rivalry
Rennes — a broader western France derby
watch: The quintessential overachiever's story — a club that punches wildly above its budget and has made a habit of shocking France's establishment.
lastSeason: Finished 12th (39 points), a step back after their Champions League season, but clear of trouble.

name: Toulouse FC
identity: The 'Violets' of southern France — a club reborn under Red Bull ownership in 2020 that has become one of Ligue 1's most analytically-driven, forward-thinking operations.
history: Founded in 1970 after the collapse of an earlier Toulouse club, TFC spent decades as a yo-yo club between divisions. The 2020 acquisition by Red Bull transformed the club's recruitment and analytics, and the model produced a stunning 2023 Coupe de France win over Nantes — the club's first major trophy. Toulouse has since established itself as a stable, smartly-run Ligue 1 club.
rivals: Bordeaux — the Derby de la Garonne, a historic southern France rivalry (currently less active with Bordeaux's relegation)
watch: A Red Bull-owned club applying the data-driven model in France — interesting for fans who want to see how analytics-first football works outside the mainstream.
lastSeason: Finished 9th (45 points), a comfortable mid-table campaign.

name: Le Havre AC
nick: Les Ciel et Marine (Sky and Navy)
honours: No top-flight league titles; 1 Coupe de France (1959)
identity: The oldest football club in mainland France (founded 1872), Le Havre is a port-city institution and a quietly elite academy that has shaped some of the most important players in French football history.
history: Founded in 1872 by English expatriates in the Normandy port of Le Havre, the club is the oldest registered football club in France. It spent decades in the lower divisions but built one of the country's most respected academies, producing Paul Pogba, Riyad Mahrez, and Benjamin Mendy among others. After a long Ligue 1 absence, Le Havre returned to the top flight in 2023.
rivals: Caen — the Derby Normand, a cross-region Normandy rivalry
watch: If you want the earliest roots of French football and one of its most productive academies, this is the oldest story in the country.
lastSeason: Finished 14th (35 points), grinding out another season of top-flight survival.

name: AJ Auxerre
honours: 1 French league title (1996), 4 Coupe de France
identity: A small-town Burgundy club that punched astronomically above its weight for decades under one of French football's longest-serving managers — a model of sustained overachievement.
history: Founded in 1905, Auxerre's entire modern identity was shaped by Guy Roux, who managed the club for over 40 years (1961–2000, 2001–05), guiding it from the amateur divisions to a 1996 Ligue 1 title and four Coupe de France wins. The club's academy produced Eric Cantona, Laurent Blanc, and Philippe Mexès. After years in Ligue 2, Auxerre returned to Ligue 1 in 2024.
rivals: Dijon — the Burgundy derby (historical)
Lyon — a broader regional rivalry
watch: The ultimate small-town success story — Guy Roux's 40-year reign is one of football's most remarkable managerial tales, and the academy's alumni list is extraordinary.
lastSeason: Finished 15th (34 points), avoiding relegation late in the season.

name: Angers SCO
honours: No major national trophies; 1 Coupe de France final (2017)
identity: A western France club named after one of football's greatest-ever players — its stadium bears Raymond Kopa's name — and a club that embodies the patient, modest provincial tradition.
history: Founded in 1919, Angers spent decades in the lower divisions before establishing itself as a Ligue 1 regular from 2015. The club reached the 2017 Coupe de France final (lost to PSG) and named its stadium after Angers-born Raymond Kopa, the 1950s Real Madrid legend and Ballon d'Or winner. The club has oscillated between Ligue 1 and Ligue 2 in recent years, returning to the top flight in 2024.
rivals: Nantes — a western France regional rivalry
Rennes — a broader Brittany/western derby
watch: A modest provincial club with a genuine football-god connection — worth following for the Raymond Kopa legacy alone.
lastSeason: Finished 13th (36 points) and secured survival; Esteban Lepaul starred before a mid-season move to Rennes.

name: Columbus Crew
identity: One of MLS's founding clubs and its most tactically modern operation — a team that won two MLS Cups in four years under a possession-first philosophy, proving that the league rewards sophistication, not just spending.
history: A founding MLS member in 1996, the Crew survived a near-relocation to Austin in 2017 thanks to a fan-led 'Save the Crew' campaign, then won MLS Cup in 2020 and 2023 under Caleb Porter and Wilfried Nancy respectively. Nancy's possession-based system made Columbus one of the most analytically admired teams in the league, and the 2023 title was among the most dominant in MLS history.
rivals: FC Cincinnati — the Hell is Real Derby, Ohio's fiercest rivalry
Toronto FC — a long-standing Eastern Conference rivalry
watch: If you want the club that proves MLS can reward tactical sophistication over spending, Wilfried Nancy's Columbus is the league's most compelling watch.
lastSeason: Finished 7th in the East (54 points) and made the playoffs, still one of the league's most admired possession sides.

name: Philadelphia Union
identity: A club built around one of MLS's best academies and a fiercely loyal, blue-collar Philadelphia fanbase — the league's most reliable pipeline of American talent.
history: Founded in 2008 and entering MLS in 2010, the Union built its identity around the Philadelphia-area youth soccer pipeline, developing players like Brenden Aaronson, Mark McKenzie, and Paxten Aaronson through its academy. The club won the 2020 Supporters' Shield and reached the 2022 MLS Cup final, consistently competing at the top of the Eastern Conference on a modest budget.
rivals: DC United — the I-95 Derby, a Mid-Atlantic rivalry
New York Red Bulls — a broader Eastern Conference rivalry
watch: If you want to follow the next generation of US national team talent before they go to Europe, Philadelphia's academy is the most productive in the league.
lastSeason: Won the 2025 Supporters' Shield as the league's best regular-season team (66 points) before falling in the playoffs; Kai Wagner and Jakob Glesnes both made the MLS Best XI.
keyPlayers: Kai Wagner — 2025 MLS Best XI full-back
Jakob Glesnes — 2025 MLS Best XI centre-back

name: Austin FC
identity: Texas's capital-city club, built around a distinctive oak-and-green identity and one of MLS's most enthusiastic new fanbases in one of America's fastest-growing cities.
history: Founded in 2018 and entering MLS in 2021, Austin FC sold out its season tickets before playing a game, built Q2 Stadium as a purpose-built soccer venue, and made the playoffs in only its second season (2022). The club's 'Los Verdes' supporters' group and the city's rapid growth have made it one of the league's most promising expansion markets, though on-field results have been mixed.
rivals: FC Dallas — the Texas Derby, a state rivalry
Houston Dynamo — the broader Texas rivalry
watch: A club in one of America's most dynamic cities with a green-inflected identity — a good pick for fans who want to grow with a young club.
lastSeason: Finished 6th in the West (47 points) and qualified for the playoffs.

name: FC Dallas
identity: One of MLS's oldest clubs and its premier academy — a Texas club that has consistently produced top American talent while operating in one of the league's most challenging youth-development markets.
history: A founding MLS member in 1996 (as the Dallas Burn), FC Dallas built one of the league's most productive academies, producing Weston McKennie, Ricardo Pepi, and Jesús Ferreira. The club won the 2016 Supporters' Shield and two US Open Cups, but has consistently struggled to convert regular-season quality into MLS Cup runs, making it a club defined by both its academy output and its trophy frustration.
rivals: Houston Dynamo — the Texas Derby (El Capitán cannon trophy)
Austin FC — the newer Texas rivalry
watch: If you want the club that produces the most American talent per dollar, FC Dallas's academy is the most consistent pipeline to the US national team.
lastSeason: Finished 7th in the West (44 points) and squeaked into the playoffs.

name: Houston Dynamo FC
identity: A Texas club with a proud back-to-back MLS Cup era (2006–07) that has spent the years since searching for sustained relevance in one of America's most sports-competitive cities.
history: Founded in 2005 as the San Jose Earthquakes relocated to Houston, the Dynamo won MLS Cups in their first two seasons (2006, 2007) under Dominic Kinnear, establishing an early identity as one of the league's most successful expansion clubs. The club built Shell Energy Stadium in 2012 as a soccer-specific venue, but the years since the early titles have been inconsistent, with occasional playoff runs and a 2023 US Open Cup win.
rivals: FC Dallas — the Texas Derby
Austin FC — a newer Texas rivalry
watch: A club with a genuine early-dynasty pedigree that's trying to rebuild — interesting for fans who like the challenge of restoring faded glory.
lastSeason: Finished 12th in the West (37 points) and missed the playoffs.

name: San Jose Earthquakes
identity: A Bay Area club with one of MLS's earliest dynasties and a small-stadium, underdog identity in one of America's most expensive housing markets.
history: A founding MLS member in 1996, the Earthquakes won MLS Cups in 2001 and 2003 under Frank Yallop and Landon Donovan, establishing one of the league's first genuine winning cultures. The club relocated to Houston in 2005 (becoming the Dynamo) but was re-established in 2008, building PayPal Park as a compact soccer-specific stadium. The years since have been lean, with the club consistently near the bottom of the Western Conference.
rivals: LA Galaxy — the California Clásico, one of MLS's oldest rivalries
Seattle Sounders — a broader Pacific Coast rivalry
watch: If you want MLS's original underdog — a club with two titles, a tiny stadium, and a loyal Bay Area fanbase despite years of struggle — this is the pick.
lastSeason: Finished 10th in the West (41 points), just missing the playoffs on tiebreakers.

name: Colorado Rapids
stadium: Dick's Sporting Goods Park, Commerce City, CO
identity: A founding MLS club with a single, surprise MLS Cup title — a mountain-West club that has operated mostly in the league's middle tier with a loyal regional following.
history: A founding MLS member in 1996, the Rapids won a surprise MLS Cup in 2010 under Gary Smith, their only major trophy. The club built Dick's Sporting Goods Park in 2007 as a soccer-specific stadium, one of the league's early such venues. The Rapids have consistently operated as a budget-conscious club, developing players and trading for value, with occasional playoff runs but rarely challenging for the top.
rivals: Real Salt Lake — the Rocky Mountain Cup, MLS's most geographically elevated rivalry
Sporting KC — a broader Western Conference rivalry
watch: If you want MLS's most mountain-state club — altitude, a single surprise title, and a patient regional identity — this is the league's only Colorado representative.
lastSeason: Finished 11th in the West (41 points) and missed out on the playoffs.

name: Real Salt Lake
identity: A Utah club with a Spanish-naming nod to Real Madrid and a 2009 MLS Cup win that proved a small-market club could contend — one of MLS's most consistent overachievers on a budget.
history: Founded in 2004 and entering MLS in 2005, Real Salt Lake won the 2009 MLS Cup under Jason Kreis, becoming one of the first small-market expansion clubs to claim a title. The club built America First Field in 2008 and developed a reputation for smart, budget-conscious roster building, consistently making the playoffs through the 2010s. RSL reached the 2013 MLS Cup final and remains a competitive Western Conference presence.
rivals: Colorado Rapids — the Rocky Mountain Cup
Sporting KC — a broader Western vs Central rivalry
watch: A club that proved MLS titles don't require big-market spending — RSL's 2009 run is one of the league's original underdog championship stories.
lastSeason: Finished 9th in the West (41 points), grabbing the final playoff spot on tiebreakers.

name: Minnesota United FC
honours: No major MLS trophies; consistent playoff presence
identity: An Upper Midwest club built on a distinctive 'Loons' identity and one of MLS's best purpose-built stadiums — bringing top-flight soccer back to a region that hadn't had it since the NASL era.
history: Tracing roots to the 1970s Minnesota Kicks and later the Minnesota Thunder, Minnesota United entered MLS in 2017 as an expansion club, playing initially at a shared facility before opening Allianz Field in 2019. The club has been a consistent playoff contender in the Western Conference, developing a strong regional following in a state with deep soccer history, including the 1970s NASL-era Kicks that drew huge crowds.
rivals: Sporting KC — a Midwest regional rivalry
Portland Timbers — a broader Western Conference rivalry
watch: If you want MLS's Upper Midwest identity — a cold-weather, loyal fanbase with one of the league's nicest mid-sized stadiums — this is the pick.
lastSeason: Finished 4th in the West (58 points) and made the playoffs; goalkeeper Dayne St. Clair earned an MLS Best XI selection.
keyPlayers: Dayne St. Clair — 2025 MLS Best XI goalkeeper

name: Charlotte FC
stadium: Bank of America Stadium, Charlotte, NC
identity: MLS's Carolina representative, playing in one of the league's largest stadiums and backed by David Tepper — a club built for scale in one of America's fastest-growing regions.
history: Founded in 2019 and entering MLS in 2022, Charlotte FC plays in the NFL-sized Bank of America Stadium, drawing some of the league's largest crowds. The club made the playoffs in its second season (2023) and has positioned itself as MLS's gateway to the Southeast, targeting the growing Charlotte metropolitan area and the broader Carolinas.
rivals: Atlanta United — the Southeast regional rivalry
Nashville SC — a newer Southern rivalry
watch: If you want a club with big-stadium scale and ambition in America's Southeast, Charlotte is MLS's newest bet on the Carolinas.
lastSeason: Finished 4th in the East (59 points) — the club's best season yet — and qualified for the playoffs.

name: Nashville SC
honours: No major trophies; consistent playoff presence from 2021
identity: A Music City club with one of MLS's best purpose-built stadiums and a reputation for pragmatic, defensively-organized football — one of the league's most successful quick-expansion stories.
history: Founded in 2016 as a lower-division club, Nashville SC entered MLS in 2020 and immediately made the playoffs in its first three seasons, a rare feat for an expansion club. The club opened GEODIS Park in 2022, the largest soccer-specific stadium in MLS (30,000 seats), and built its early reputation on Gary Smith's defensively solid system and the goalscoring of Hany Mukhtar.
rivals: Charlotte FC — a newer Southern rivalry
Atlanta United — a broader Southeast regional rivalry
watch: If you want a club that proved expansion success is possible through defensive discipline and smart recruitment rather than star power, Nashville is the model.
lastSeason: Finished 6th in the East (54 points) and made the playoffs; striker Sam Surridge was an MVP finalist.
keyPlayers: Sam Surridge — 2025 MLS MVP finalist

name: Orlando City SC
identity: Central Florida's MLS club, built on a purple-clad identity and one of the league's most passionate Latin American-influenced fanbases — Florida's second club after the original Tampa Bay Mutiny era.
history: Tracing roots to a USL Pro championship in 2011–2014, Orlando City entered MLS in 2015 with Kaká as its first Designated Player, immediately drawing large crowds. The club built Inter&Co Stadium (originally Exploria Stadium) as a downtown soccer-specific venue and won its first major trophy, the 2022 US Open Cup, under Óscar Pareja, establishing itself as a consistent Eastern Conference contender.
rivals: Inter Miami — the Florida rivalry, sharpened by Messi-era narratives
Atlanta United — a broader Southeast regional rivalry
watch: If you want Florida's original MLS identity — purple, passionate, and Latin American-influenced — Orlando predates Inter Miami and has the deeper regional roots.
lastSeason: Finished 9th in the East (53 points) to reach the playoffs; Alex Freeman broke out with a 2025 MLS Best XI selection.
keyPlayers: Alex Freeman — 2025 MLS Best XI defender

name: D.C. United
identity: MLS's first dynasty — the club that won four of the first nine MLS Cups and established the league's earliest template for sustained excellence.
history: A founding MLS member in 1996, D.C. United won the first two MLS Cups (1996, 1997) under Bruce Arena and added two more (1999, 2004), establishing the league's first dominant era with players like Jaime Moreno, Marco Etcheverry, and Eddie Pope. The club opened Audi Field in 2018 after years at RFK Stadium, but the post-dynasty years have been lean, with D.C. becoming one of the league's more struggling original franchises despite its early pedigree.
rivals: New York Red Bulls — the Atlantic Cup, one of MLS's oldest rivalries
Philadelphia Union — the I-95 Derby
watch: If you want MLS's original dynasty and its earliest great players — a club with four titles that's now defined by the gap between past glory and present struggle — this is the pick.
lastSeason: Finished bottom of the East (15th, 26 points), extending a long rebuild.

name: Chicago Fire FC
identity: A club named after the Great Chicago Fire of 1871 that won an MLS Cup in its inaugural season — a Midwestern club with one of the league's most distinctive founding stories and one of its longest trophy droughts.
history: Founded in 1997 and entering MLS in 1998, the Fire won the MLS Cup in their very first season under Bob Bradley, a feat matched by only one other expansion club. The club also won four US Open Cups, establishing an early pedigree. The years since have been defined by a long title drought, stadium challenges at SeatGeek Stadium in Bridgeview (away from downtown Chicago), and multiple rebranding efforts that have alienated portions of the fanbase.
rivals: FC Cincinnati — a newer Midwest rivalry
Columbus Crew — a broader Midwest regional rivalry
watch: If you want the club with the most dramatic gap between a brilliant founding story (MLS Cup in year one) and a long, painful present, Chicago is MLS's longest-running frustration story.
lastSeason: Finished 8th in the East (53 points) to return to the playoffs after a long absence.

name: New England Revolution
identity: A club owned by Robert Kraft (New England Patriots) that set an MLS single-season points record in 2021 — a team that has consistently competed but never won MLS Cup, making it the league's most successful trophy-less club.
history: A founding MLS member in 1996, the Revolution reached three MLS Cup finals (2002, 2005, 2006) under Steve Nicol, losing all three, establishing a reputation as the league's nearly-men. The 2021 season under Bruce Arena produced a single-season points record (73) and a Supporters' Shield, but another playoff disappointment. The club plays at the NFL-sized Gillette Stadium and has explored building a soccer-specific venue in Boston.
rivals: New York Red Bulls — the I-95 Northeast rivalry
DC United — a founding-era Eastern Conference rivalry
watch: If you want the club that proves you can be consistently good and never quite win the big one, the Revs are MLS's most persistent almost-story.
lastSeason: Finished 11th in the East (36 points), missing the playoffs in a rebuilding year.

name: Toronto FC
identity: MLS's first Canadian club and the only one to win a domestic double — a club whose 2017 treble-chasing season set the league's single-season points record before Columbus broke it.
history: Founded in 2005 and entering MLS in 2007, Toronto FC endured a decade of struggle before a 2016–2018 peak under Greg Vanney, winning a 2017 MLS Cup and Supporters' Shield double and reaching the CONCACAF Champions League final. The club's BMO Field on the Exhibition Place grounds is one of MLS's best downtown soccer venues, and its fanbase — including the Red Patch Boys — is among the league's most established supporter cultures.
rivals: Montreal (CF Montréal) — the Canadian Derby (401 Derby), MLS's only all-Canadian rivalry
Columbus Crew — a bitter Eastern Conference rivalry sharpened by playoff battles
watch: If you want MLS's most successful Canadian club and its 2017 peak — the closest the league came to a true treble — Toronto's BMO Field is one of the best matchday experiences.
lastSeason: Finished 12th in the East (32 points), another season outside the playoff places.

name: Vancouver Whitecaps FC
identity: A Pacific Northwest club with roots in the 1970s NASL — one of MLS's most geographically dramatic settings, playing in a domed stadium against a mountain backdrop.
history: The Whitecaps trace to the 1974 NASL franchise, which won the 1979 Soccer Bowl, before the modern MLS club was established in 2009 and entered the league in 2011. The club plays at BC Place, a domed stadium with a retractable roof, and has been a consistent if modest Western Conference presence, winning multiple Canadian Championships and reaching the 2023 Leagues Cup semifinal.
rivals: Seattle Sounders — the Cascadia Cup rivalry, one of MLS's oldest regional traditions
Portland Timbers — the broader Cascadia Cup three-way rivalry
watch: If you want the Pacific Northwest's Canadian side and the Cascadia Cup tradition, Vancouver has the most dramatic stadium setting in MLS.
lastSeason: Reached the 2025 MLS Cup final (losing 3-1 to Inter Miami) after finishing 2nd in the West; Sebastian Berhalter and Tristan Blackmon made the MLS Best XI.
keyPlayers: Sebastian Berhalter — 2025 MLS Best XI midfielder
Tristan Blackmon — 2025 MLS Best XI defender

name: St. Louis City SC
identity: MLS's club in America's most historically soccer-rich city — St. Louis produced more US World Cup players than any other American city, and City SC is the realization of a century of soccer culture.
history: St. Louis is America's foundational soccer city, producing the core of the 1950 US World Cup team that beat England and more US internationals than any other city. Despite this, top-flight soccer didn't return until St. Louis City SC entered MLS in 2023, immediately topping the Western Conference in a stunning expansion season. CityPark, a downtown soccer-specific venue, sold out immediately and the club's red-and-blue identity connected instantly with the region's deep soccer heritage.
rivals: Sporting KC — the I-70 Derby, a Midwest regional rivalry
Chicago Fire — a broader Midwest rivalry
watch: If you want the club that represents America's deepest soccer-history city — a place that shaped US football for a century before finally getting a pro team — this is MLS's most overdue story.
lastSeason: Finished 13th in the West (32 points), a fall from their headline-making debut season.

name: San Diego FC
identity: MLS's 30th and newest club, backed by the Sycuan Band of the Kumeyaay Nation — the first Native American tribe to own a stake in a major US professional sports franchise — and representing America's most soccer-native city without a top-flight team.
history: Founded in 2023 and entering MLS in 2025 as the league's 30th franchise, San Diego FC is co-owned by the Sycuan Band of the Kumeyaay Nation and British-Egyptian billionaire Mohamed Mansour. The club's founding reflects San Diego's deep soccer culture — a border city with strong Latin American influence and consistent US national team player production — and aims to represent the binational identity of the region.
rivals: LA Galaxy — a Southern California rivalry, sharpened by geographic proximity
LAFC — a broader Southern California regional competition
watch: If you want to be there from the very beginning of a club — and support one with genuinely groundbreaking Native American ownership — San Diego is MLS's newest and most culturally distinctive project.
lastSeason: Topped the Western Conference (63 points) in their inaugural 2025 season — a stunning debut — led by MVP finalist Anders Dreyer.
keyPlayers: Anders Dreyer — 2025 MLS Best XI forward and MVP finalist

name: CF Montréal
identity: Quebec's MLS club, rooted in a French-speaking city with deep European football culture — a club that has struggled with identity through rebranding but retains a loyal, bilingual supporter base.
history: Founded in 1992 as the Montreal Impact, the club spent two decades in lower divisions — including the NASL — before entering MLS in 2012. The club reached the 2015 CONCACAF Champions League final under Frank Klopas, a notable run for an MLS side. A 2021 rebrand from 'Impact' to 'CF Montréal' under new ownership alienated much of the fanbase, and the club has navigated tension between its French-Quebecer identity and MLS's broader American commercial frame.
rivals: Toronto FC — the Canadian Derby (401 Derby), MLS's only all-Canadian rivalry
Vancouver Whitecaps — the broader Canadian rivalry
watch: If you want MLS's most European-influenced club culture — French-language, Quebec-rooted, with a CONCACAF final run — Montreal is the league's most distinctively non-American identity.
lastSeason: Finished 13th in the East (28 points) in a difficult, rebuilding campaign.

name: CF Pachuca
honours: 7 Liga MX titles, 1 CONCACAF Champions Cup, 1 Copa Sudamericana
identity: Mexico's oldest football club (founded 1892 by Cornish miners) and one of its most internationally successful — a small-city club that has won continental trophies and pioneered football education through its global academy network.
history: Founded in 1892 by Cornish miners in the silver-mining town of Pachuca, the club is Mexico's oldest registered football team. After decades in the lower divisions, Pachuca rose dramatically in the late 1990s–2000s under the ownership of Grupo Pachuca, winning seven Liga MX titles and becoming the first Mexican club to win a South American competition (the 2006 Copa Sudamericana). The club's university and global academy network (including links to Everton and Sudamérica) make it one of Mexico's most innovative operations.
rivals: Club América — a rivalry sharpened by both being historically dominant Mexico City-adjacent clubs
Toluca — a regional central Mexico derby
watch: If you want Mexico's oldest club and one of its most internationally accomplished — a small-city club that punches far above its weight continentally — Pachuca is a unique story.
lastSeason: Finished 4th in the Clausura 2026 general table and reached the Liguilla quarterfinals.

name: Club Santos Laguna
identity: A northern Mexico club from the Comarca Lagunera region — a relatively young club (1983) that became one of Liga MX's most consistent title contenders despite its modest origins.
history: Founded in 1983 in Torreón, Coahuila, Santos Laguna rose from the second division to become one of Liga MX's most reliable contenders, winning six league titles across the 1990s, 2000s, and 2010s. The club built the modern Estadio Nuevo Corona in 2009 and developed a reputation for attacking football and a loyal regional following in the Laguna region, a relatively isolated northern market. The club has struggled more recently but retains a strong regional identity.
rivals: Cruz Azul — a rivalry sharpened by several dramatic championship finals between the two
Monterrey — a northern Mexico regional rivalry
watch: If you want a northern Mexican club with genuine title pedigree but less of the media spotlight of the bigger clubs, Santos is a consistently competitive regional story.
lastSeason: Missed the Clausura 2026 Liguilla, continuing a lean spell for the northern club.

name: Club Querétaro
honours: No Liga MX titles; 1 Copa MX (2016)
identity: A central Mexican club known for its 'White Roosters' nickname and a single Copa MX title — a modest club that has survived multiple ownership changes and a major stadium tragedy.
history: Founded in 1950, Querétaro spent decades in Mexico's lower divisions before establishing itself as a Liga MX regular from the 2000s. The club won its only major trophy, the 2016 Copa MX, under Jaime Lozano. The Estadio Corregidora, one of Mexico's most historic football venues (built for the 1986 World Cup), was the site of a 2022 stadium brawl that led to sweeping Liga MX fan-identity regulations — an event that reshaped Mexican football's crowd-control policies.
rivals: Atlas — a central Mexico regional rivalry
San Luis — a broader Bajío region derby
watch: If you want the club at the center of one of Liga MX's most important recent off-field stories — the 2022 incident that changed Mexican stadium policy — Querétaro is a club shaped by adversity.
lastSeason: Missed the Clausura 2026 Liguilla, another season in the lower half of the table.

name: Club Atlas
identity: Guadalajara's other club — the working-class, academically-rooted alternative to Chivas — that waited 70 years between its first and second league titles, making it one of Mexican football's most poignant patience stories.
history: Founded in 1916, Atlas is Guadalajara's oldest club and historically its more academically-oriented, working-class alternative to the globally-known Chivas. The club won its first league title in 1951 and then endured an extraordinary 70-year drought — the longest in Liga MX history — before finally winning again in the 2021 Apertura under Diego Cocca, a cathartic moment for one of Mexico's most loyal fanbases. Atlas's academy has produced numerous Mexican internationals, including Rafael Márquez and Jared Borgetti.
rivals: Chivas — the Clásico Tapatío, Guadalajara's intra-city rivalry and one of Mexico's most historic derbies
Club América — a broader historical rivalry
watch: If you want one of football's great patience stories — a club that waited 70 years between titles and then finally won — Atlas's 2021 championship is among Liga MX's most emotional moments.
lastSeason: Finished 6th in the Clausura 2026 general table and reached the Liguilla — a rare bright season for the long-suffering Guadalajara club.

name: Club Atlético de San Luis
stadium: Estadio Alfonso Lastras, San Luis Potosí
honours: No Liga MX titles; 1 Liga de Expansión MX title (promoted 2019)
identity: A relatively young central Mexican club (refounded 2013) that has established itself as a Liga MX regular — a club representing the Bajío region with a modest budget and a growing following.
history: The current Atlético de San Luis was founded in 2013 after the collapse of a previous San Luis FC, inheriting the city's football tradition. The club won promotion to Liga MX in 2019 and has since established itself as a stable top-flight presence, playing at the Estadio Alfonso Lastras. Despite its modest budget and lack of major trophies, the club has developed a loyal regional following in San Luis Potosí, a state capital in central Mexico.
rivals: Querétaro — the Bajío region rivalry
Atlas — a broader central Mexico competition
watch: If you want one of Liga MX's newest and most modest clubs — a regional team without the history or budget of Mexico's giants — San Luis is a genuine underdog in a big-spending league.
lastSeason: Missed the Clausura 2026 Liguilla, but striker João Pedro won the tournament's top-scorer crown (14 goals) — his second straight Golden Boot after also topping Apertura 2025.
keyPlayers: João Pedro — back-to-back Liga MX top scorer (Apertura 2025 & Clausura 2026)

name: Club Puebla
identity: A central Mexican club with a distinctive blue-and-white striped identity and a proud 1980s title era — a club that has survived financial crises to remain a Liga MX fixture.
history: Founded in 1944, Puebla won two Liga MX titles (1983 under Manuel Lapuente, 1990 under Mario Vargas) and established itself as one of central Mexico's traditional clubs. The Estadio Cuauhtémoc, one of Mexico's most distinctive venues with its facade of colorful murals, has been the club's home since 1968. The club has endured multiple financial crises and ownership changes, including a 2014 re-foundation, but retains a loyal following in the Puebla state capital.
rivals: Club América — a historical rivalry sharpened by 1980s title battles
Veracruz — a defunct but historically significant regional rivalry
watch: If you want a traditional central Mexican club with a distinctive stadium and a proud 1980s history — one that has survived more crises than most — Puebla is a resilient regional story.
lastSeason: Missed the Clausura 2026 Liguilla, finishing among the bottom clubs of the general table.

name: Club de Fútbol Juárez
honours: No Liga MX titles; 1 Liga de Expansión MX title (promoted 2019)
identity: A border-city club representing Ciudad Juárez — one of the most distinctive urban contexts in Mexican football, with a binational identity straddling the US-Mexico border opposite El Paso, Texas.
history: Founded in 2019 after the dissolution of a previous Juárez club, FC Juárez won promotion to Liga MX in its first year and has established itself as a top-flight regular. The club represents Ciudad Juárez, a major manufacturing city on the US border with a complex social history, and draws supporters from both sides of the border. The Estadio Benito Juárez, while modest, has developed a growing matchday culture.
rivals: Santos Laguna — a northern Mexico regional rivalry
Monterrey — a broader northern Mexico competition
watch: If you want Liga MX's most binational club — a border city whose fans cross international lines to attend matches — Juárez represents one of football's most distinctive geographic contexts.
lastSeason: Missed the Clausura 2026 Liguilla, though the border club produced some memorable results including a home win over champions-in-waiting.

name: Club Tijuana
identity: A border-city club named after the Xoloitzcuintle dog breed — the quickest rise from founding to Liga MX title in modern Mexican football, representing the Baja California cross-border corridor.
history: Founded in 2007 in Tijuana, Baja California, the club achieved an extraordinary rapid rise: promotion to Liga MX in 2011 and a Liga MX title in the 2012 Apertura under Antonio Mohamed — one of the fastest expansions-to-titles in football history. The club's cross-border location (opposite San Diego) has given it a genuinely binational fanbase, and its 'Xolos' identity — referencing the hairless Mexican dog breed — is one of Liga MX's most distinctive brand stories.
rivals: Club América — a rivalry sharpened by 2010s competitive encounters
Santos Laguna — a northern Mexico regional rivalry
watch: If you want the club with the most distinctive mascot in football (a dog breed) and the fastest title-rise story in Mexican football, Tijuana's binational border identity is unique.
lastSeason: Narrowly missed the Clausura 2026 Liguilla, finishing 9th in the general table, just outside the top eight.

name: Club Necaxa
honours: 3 Liga MX titles, 2 CONCACAF Champions Cups
identity: A club originally founded by the Mexican electric company — hence 'Los Rayos' (Lightning) — that was once one of Mexican football's most successful clubs and moved across states to survive.
history: Founded in 1923 by employees of the Compañía de Luz (Light Company), Necaxa won three Liga MX titles in the 1930s and two CONCACAF Champions Cups in the 1990s, when the club had a golden era under Raúl Arias. The club relocated from Mexico City to Aguascalientes in 2003 to survive financially, a move that preserved the club but severed it from its original home. The club has oscillated between divisions but retains a respected historical pedigree.
rivals: Club América — a historic Mexico City rivalry from Necaxa's capital-city era
Atlas — a broader central Mexico competition
watch: If you want one of Mexican football's oldest and most historically decorated clubs — now in a smaller city after a relocation — Necaxa carries a pedigree that outstrips its current status.
lastSeason: Missed the Clausura 2026 Liguilla, finishing in the lower reaches of the general table.

name: Sunderland AFC
identity: One of English football's great sleeping giants — a one-club city with a fanatical, oversized following that packed the Stadium of Light even in League One, finally back where its support demands it belongs.
history: Sunderland were founding members of the old First Division's elite in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, winning six league titles and becoming known as the 'Team of All the Talents.' The club's finest modern hour came in 1973, when Second Division Sunderland shocked mighty Leeds United to win the FA Cup, with goalkeeper Jim Montgomery's double save entering folklore. Sunderland left Roker Park for the Stadium of Light in 1997, but the 21st century brought a painful cycle of relegations, culminating in years stuck in League One while still drawing crowds bigger than most Championship clubs. A dramatic 2025 play-off final win over Sheffield United, capped by a stoppage-time winner from teenager Tom Watson, finally ended an eight-year top-flight exile.
rivals: Newcastle United — the Tyne-Wear derby, one of England's fiercest and most historic rivalries
Middlesbrough — a regional North East rivalry with real needle
watch: Watch for the noise — the Stadium of Light's atmosphere, inherited from the legendary Roker Roar, remains one of the loudest and most passionate in English football regardless of league position.
lastSeason: Enjoyed a superb return to the Premier League in 2025-26, finishing 7th (54 points) to qualify for the Europa League; Granit Xhaka was voted Player of the Season.
keyPlayers: Granit Xhaka — 2025-26 Player of the Season

name: Leeds United
honours: 3 English league titles, 1 FA Cup, 1 League Cup, 2 Inter-Cities Fairs Cups
identity: A one-city footballing institution whose identity was forged by Don Revie's ruthless, dominant side of the late '60s and '70s, then reforged by Marcelo Bielsa's chaotic, high-intensity revival — 'Leeds is Leeds' is both a boast and a warning.
history: Formed in 1919 from the ashes of Leeds City, the club became a genuine European power under Don Revie, winning two league titles, an FA Cup, a League Cup, and two Fairs Cups between 1964 and 1974 with a side built on Billy Bremner, Jack Charlton, and Norman Hunter. Leeds reached a Champions League semi-final in 2001 but financial overreach triggered a collapse into administration and a slide as low as League One by 2007. Marcelo Bielsa's arrival in 2018 sparked a stunning transformation, returning the club to the Premier League in 2020 with a thrilling, relentless pressing style that captivated neutrals. After yo-yoing between the Championship and top flight in the mid-2020s, Leeds were promoted again for 2025-26 and held their place into 2026-27.
rivals: Manchester United — bitter rivals since the Revie/Busby era, intensified by Yorkshire-Lancashire history
Millwall — one of English football's most notorious hooligan-era rivalries
Huddersfield Town and Bradford City — fierce local Yorkshire derbies
watch: Watch for intensity over polish — whichever manager is in charge, Leeds sides are built to press, run, and make Elland Road as hostile as possible for visitors.
lastSeason: Survived their return to the Premier League in 2025-26, finishing 14th (47 points); Ethan Ampadu was voted Player of the Season.
keyPlayers: Ethan Ampadu — captain and 2025-26 Player of the Season

name: Coventry City
identity: English football's great romantic underdog story of the modern era — a club that spent a quarter-century wandering the lower divisions and even its own city's stadium ownership limbo before clawing back to the top flight.
history: Coventry City's one shining moment came in 1987, when underdogs managed by John Sirrell and George Curtis stunned Tottenham Hotspur 3-2 in the FA Cup final, still the club's only major honour. The Sky Blues spent over three decades in the top flight from the 1960s onward under visionary chairman Jimmy Hill, who pioneered all-seater stadiums and shirt sponsorship, before relegation in 2001 began a long decline. A bitter, years-long dispute over stadium ownership forced the club to play home games away from Coventry entirely at points, deepening supporter frustration even as the team bounced between League One and the Championship. Under Mark Robins the club rebuilt steadily, reaching Championship play-offs repeatedly before finally winning promotion to the Premier League in 2026 — its first top-flight football in 25 years — with the CBS Arena ownership dispute finally resolved in 2025.
rivals: Leicester City — the M69 derby, the club's most fiercely contested modern rivalry
Aston Villa — a traditional Midlands rivalry
Birmingham City — a secondary local rivalry
watch: Watch for a club still finding its top-flight feet after 25 years away — expect a tight, well-organized underdog set-up built on team spirit rather than star names.
lastSeason: Won the 2025-26 EFL Championship title to earn automatic promotion to the Premier League — a return to the top flight for the first time since 2001.

name: Ipswich Town
honours: 1 English league title (1962), 1 FA Cup (1978), 1 UEFA Cup (1981)
identity: A homely East Anglian club whose golden age under Sir Bobby Robson produced one of English football's great overachieving European runs, now living through a dizzying modern yo-yo between the Championship and Premier League.
history: Ipswich Town's finest era came under Bobby Robson, who led the club to the 1978 FA Cup with a win over Arsenal and then a stunning 1981 UEFA Cup triumph over AZ Alkmaar, having also won the league title in 1962 under Alf Ramsey. The club settled into mid-table top-flight obscurity for decades before relegation in 1986 began a long absence from England's elite. Under Kieran McKenna, Ipswich produced one of modern football's most remarkable turnarounds: back-to-back promotions from League One to the Premier League by 2024, a whirlwind ride for a club that had spent over two decades outside the top two divisions. The story took another twist when Ipswich were relegated straight back down in 2025, only to bounce immediately back to the Premier League for 2026-27 — a genuine yo-yo club living out both the thrill of promotion and the sting of relegation in consecutive seasons.
rivals: Norwich City — the East Anglian derby, one of England's most fiercely contested regional rivalries
Colchester United — a smaller but spirited Suffolk-Essex rivalry
watch: Watch a club riding real momentum but with a very recent taste of relegation — Ipswich's yo-yo run makes their Premier League survival battle genuinely uncertain from week one.
lastSeason: Finished 2nd in the 2025-26 EFL Championship to win automatic promotion, bouncing straight back to the Premier League at the first attempt.

name: Hull City
honours: No major honours; 2014 FA Cup finalists
identity: A club without a single major trophy to its name, defined instead by its distinctive amber-and-black stripes and a never-say-die run to the 2014 FA Cup final that remains the high point of its history.
history: Founded in 1904, Hull City spent most of the 20th century outside the top flight, only reaching the English top division for the first time in 2008 via a play-off final winner from local hero Dean Windass. Under Steve Bruce, the Tigers reached their first FA Cup final in 2014, racing into a 2-0 lead against Arsenal before losing 3-2 after extra time — still the club's signature modern achievement. Hull's Premier League stays have historically been brief, with the club bouncing between the second and top tiers and enduring ownership turbulence under the Allam family, including a controversial attempt to rebrand as 'Hull City Tigers.' After nine years away, promotion back to the Premier League returns Hull to the top flight for 2026-27.
rivals: Leeds United — a fierce Yorkshire rivalry
Sheffield United and Sheffield Wednesday — traditional Yorkshire derbies
watch: Watch for a club with nothing to prove and everything to gain — Hull's amber stripes and gritty underdog identity make every top-flight point feel like a small triumph.
lastSeason: Won the 2025-26 Championship play-off final 1-0 against Middlesbrough — Oli McBurnie striking in the 95th minute — to clinch promotion to the Premier League.
keyPlayers: Oli McBurnie — scored the 95th-minute play-off final winner that sealed promotion

name: Levante UD
honours: 3x Segunda División titles (2003-04, 2016-17, 2024-25), Copa de la España Libre 1937
identity: Valencia's older, scrappier club — the city's original football team, permanently living in the shadow of its more decorated neighbor while riding an exhausting elevator between La Liga and the Segunda.
history: Founded in 1909, Levante actually predates Valencia CF by a decade, inheriting the 'Granota' (Frog) nickname from a 1939 merger with Gimnàstic FC. The club spent most of the 20th century outside the top flight, only establishing itself as a fixture of Spanish football in the 21st century, including a surprising run to the Europa League in 2011-12 under Juan Ignacio Martínez. Levante has become notorious for its yo-yo status, bouncing between La Liga and Segunda División multiple times in the last fifteen years, most recently promoted again for 2025-26 after their third Segunda title. A young Johan Cruyff even turned out for the club in a brief twilight cameo in the early 1980s, one of the odder footnotes in Levante's long history.
rivals: Valencia CF — the lopsided Derbi Valencià, fought for over a century by the city's two clubs
Villarreal CF — the Derbi de la Comunitat for regional bragging rights
watch: A classic yo-yo club — watch for the resilience of a fanbase that keeps coming back to La Liga against the financial gravity of sharing a city with a giant.
lastSeason: Secured survival on their return to La Liga in 2025-26, finishing 16th.

name: Elche CF
identity: A modest Valencian community club forever defined by one glorious near-miss — the 1969 Copa del Rey final — and a habit of overachieving relative to its small-city resources before crashing back down.
history: Formed in 1923 from the merger of Elche's local clubs, the Franjiverdes took decades to climb into Spanish football's upper tiers, finally reaching La Liga in 1959. Their golden moment came in 1969, when Elche reached the Copa del Rey final at the Santiago Bernabéu, narrowly losing 1-0 to Athletic Bilbao — still the club's greatest achievement, commemorated in the naming of their stadium after club president Manuel Martínez Valero. Elche has since lived a feast-or-famine existence in the Spanish pyramid, suffering financial crises and even a brief expulsion to the regional leagues in 2015 due to bankruptcy, only to rebuild and return to La Liga repeatedly. The club is now back in the top flight after their most recent stint in the Segunda, continuing a pattern of resilience that defines their fanbase.
rivals: Hércules CF — the Derbi de la Provincia de Alicante, a fixture since the 1920s
Real Murcia — a historic regional rivalry dating to the late 1920s
watch: A club defined by resilience through financial near-death experiences — watch for the tight, defensively organized sides that punch above their transfer budget.
lastSeason: Comfortably retained their La Liga place in 2025-26, finishing 15th on their return to the top flight.

name: Racing de Santander
honours: 13x Campeonato Regional de Cantabria (1922-1940), founding member of La Liga (1929)
identity: Cantabria's one true club and a founding member of La Liga in 1929, whose descent into the third tier and improbable climb back to the top flight is one of Spanish football's great modern redemption stories.
history: Founded in 1913, Racing became one of the ten founding clubs of La Liga in 1929 and enjoyed a long, stable top-flight history through the 20th century, even producing a young Fernando Torres's youth-team peers and famously beating both Real Madrid and Barcelona in the same season back in 1935-36. The 2000s brought a two-decade run in La Liga, but boardroom chaos and financial mismanagement triggered back-to-back relegations in 2011-12 and 2012-13, and by the mid-2010s Racing had collapsed all the way to Segunda División B, Spanish football's third tier. What followed was a grassroots rebuilding story fueled by a fiercely loyal Cantabrian fanbase that kept filling El Sardinero even in regional obscurity, culminating in a playoff promotion back to La Liga after fourteen years away — one of the most emotional returns of the era.
rivals: Sporting Gijón — the Derbi del Norte, a fierce rivalry between Cantabria and Asturias
Real Valladolid — a historic Castilian-Cantabrian fixture
watch: Perhaps La Liga's most emotional comeback story — watch for the deafening El Sardinero atmosphere from a fanbase that never stopped showing up during fourteen years in the wilderness.
lastSeason: Won automatic promotion from the Segunda División in 2025-26, sealing a return to La Liga after a 14-year absence.

name: Deportivo de La Coruña
honours: La Liga title 2000, 2x Copa del Rey (1995, 2002), 3x Spanish Super Cup (1995, 2000, 2002)
identity: Galicia's greatest football fairy tale — a provincial club from a city of 250,000 that won La Liga in 2000 and reached a Champions League semifinal in 2004, only to collapse into the third tier two decades later.
history: Founded in 1906, Deportivo spent most of the 20th century as a middling Spanish club until the 'Super Depor' era of the 1990s and early 2000s transformed it into a genuine European power. Under manager Javier Irureta and led by stars like Djalminha, Juan Carlos Valerón, Roy Makaay, and Mauro Silva, Deportivo won the 1999-2000 La Liga title — still the smallest city ever to produce a Spanish champion at the time — and reached five straight top-three finishes. Their defining European moment came in 2004, overturning a 4-1 first-leg deficit to beat AC Milan 4-0 at Riazor in the Champions League quarterfinals, before falling to Monaco in the semifinal. The club's finances never recovered from the spending of that golden era, and a slow decline through the 2010s culminated in relegation all the way to Primera Federación (the third tier) in the early 2020s, making this return to La Liga an enormously romantic homecoming for one of Spain's most passionate, oversized fanbases.
rivals: Celta Vigo — the Derbi Galego, Spain's fiercest regional rivalry outside the big cities
Real Madrid — bitter memories from title-race controversies in the Super Depor years
watch: A romantic homecoming after collapse to the third tier — watch for the Riazor atmosphere and lingering Super Depor nostalgia driving a fanbase that punches far above the city's size.
lastSeason: Earned automatic promotion from the Segunda División in 2025-26, ending a long exile and returning the fallen giant to La Liga.

name: Málaga CF
honours: UEFA Intertoto Cup 2002, Champions League quarterfinals 2012-13
identity: A Costa del Sol club that briefly became one of the wealthiest in Europe under Qatari ownership, reaching the Champions League quarterfinals in 2013, before a spectacular financial collapse dragged it into years of administration and lower-division exile.
history: Founded in 1948 as Atlético Malagueño, the club took its current name and identity through several reformations before establishing itself as a mid-table La Liga fixture. Everything changed in 2010 when Qatari royal Sheikh Abdullah bin Nasser Al-Thani bought a majority stake and bankrolled a spending spree, assembling a squad featuring Isco, Martín Demichelis, Santi Cazorla, and Salomón Rondón under manager Manuel Pellegrini. The high point came in 2012-13, when Málaga topped a Champions League group containing AC Milan and reached the quarterfinals before losing narrowly to Borussia Dortmund — even as unpaid debts saw the club banned from European competition the following season. Isco's sale to Real Madrid in 2013 symbolized the unraveling, and Al-Thani's ownership descended into years of unpaid wages, points deductions, and legal battles, eventually forcing the club into judicial administration and a fall through the Segunda División all the way to Primera Federación before this return to the top flight.
rivals: Sevilla FC — an Andalusian rivalry sharpened by Málaga's rise and fall
Real Betis — a broader Andalusian regional fixture
watch: A cautionary tale of boom and bust — watch for lingering scars from the Al-Thani ownership crisis alongside genuine excitement about a return from the financial abyss.
lastSeason: Won promotion to La Liga through the Segunda División play-offs in 2025-26 (beating Almería), returning to the top flight after an eight-year absence.

name: Frosinone Calcio
honours: Serie B playoff/promotion 2015-16, 2018-19, 2022-23, 2025-26 (3x Serie A promotion via table, 1x via runner-up finish)
identity: A modest Ciociaria provincial club that has made a habit of yo-yoing between Serie A and Serie B, punching above its weight through shrewd recruitment and a knack for developing loanees and unknowns into first-team stars it later cashes in on.
history: Frosinone traces its roots to a 1906 amateur side but counts 1928 as its competitive founding, and spent most of the 20th century in the lower divisions before a modern revival under president Maurizio Stirpe. The club reached Serie A for the first time in 2015-16, was instantly relegated, then returned in 2018-19 and again in 2020s cycles, each stint brief but earning wider respect for its overachieving squads. Its 2023-24 top-flight campaign was built around loanee Matías Soulé, whose breakout form under manager Fabio Grosso helped fund the next rebuild after relegation. Frosinone bounced back immediately, finishing runner-up in the 2025-26 Serie B table to secure a fourth promotion to Serie A for 2026-27. The Stadio Benito Stirpe, opened in 2017 and named for the club's late honorary president, has become a symbol of the family-run project's steady modernization.
rivals: Latina Calcio — the Derby del Basso Lazio, a fierce Ciociaria-versus-Pontina regional rivalry
Lazio and Roma — occasional and lopsided but geographically close Lazio-region fixtures
watch: A perennial yo-yo club that treats every Serie A season as a chance to sell breakout talent for a profit — watch for whichever loanee or unheralded signing becomes this year's Soulé.
lastSeason: Finished 2nd in the 2025-26 Serie B to win automatic promotion back to Serie A.

name: US Sassuolo Calcio
stadium: Mapei Stadium – Città del Tricolore, Reggio Emilia
identity: A small-town Emilian club bankrolled by chemical-and-adhesives giant Mapei that built an outsized Serie A reputation on progressive, possession-based football and one of Italy's sharpest scouting-and-resale operations.
history: Founded in 1920 and shaped through mergers of local Emilian amateur sides, Sassuolo spent decades in regional obscurity until Mapei magnate Giorgio Squinzi took over as sponsor in 2002 and owner in 2003, funding a methodical climb through the divisions. Promotion to Serie B came in 2008 and to Serie A in 2013, an improbable rise for a club representing a town of roughly 40,000 people with no natural fanbase of its own, sharing the Mapei Stadium with neighboring Reggio Emilia. Under sporting director Giovanni Carnevali, Sassuolo built a model of recruiting undervalued or unheralded talent — Domenico Berardi homegrown, plus Manuel Locatelli, Gianluca Scamacca, Davide Frattesi and Giacomo Raspadori developed and later sold for big profits — while playing attractive, possession-oriented football under coaches like Roberto De Zerbi. The club was relegated to Serie B in 2024 after 11 straight years in the top flight, but won immediate promotion back by topping the 2024-25 Serie B table, returning to Serie A for 2026-27.
rivals: Modena — the Derby dell'Emilia / Derby del Panaro, a close-proximity provincial rivalry
Reggiana — a stadium-sharing-adjacent rivalry with fellow Reggio Emilia-area club
legends: Gianluca Scamacca (forward, sold to West Ham)
Giovanni Carnevali (sporting director, architect of the player-trading model)
watch: Watch the recruitment more than the results — Sassuolo's real skill is finding the next Berardi or Frattesi before anyone else notices, then playing eye-catching football while doing it.
lastSeason: Comfortably retained their Serie A status in 2025-26, finishing 11th.

name: 1. FC Köln
honours: 3 German championships (incl. inaugural 1963-64 Bundesliga title), 4 DFB-Pokal titles
identity: Cologne's carnival-city club, forever tied to a live mascot goat named Hennes and a fanbase whose devotion survives an exhausting cycle of promotion and relegation between the top two divisions.
history: Formed in 1948 from the merger of Kölner Ballspiel-Club 1901 and SpVgg Sülz 07, FC Köln became a founding member of the Bundesliga in 1963 and won its very first title in 1963-64. A golden era followed, including a league-and-cup double in 1978 and a run to the 1986 UEFA Cup final, lost narrowly to Real Madrid. Since the 1990s the club has become notorious as Germany's ultimate yo-yo team, bouncing between the Bundesliga and 2. Bundesliga more than almost any other side while somehow retaining one of the country's largest and loudest support bases. The club's live goat mascot, Hennes, has been a matchday fixture since 1950 and is inseparable from Cologne's Rhineland carnival identity.
rivals: Borussia Mönchengladbach — the Rhine derby, the club's fiercest and oldest rivalry
Fortuna Düsseldorf — a regional Rhineland rivalry
Bayer Leverkusen — a rivalry sharpened by Leverkusen's perceived corporate roots
watch: Look for the live goat mascot Hennes paraded pitchside before kickoff, and brace for a stadium atmosphere shaped as much by Cologne's carnival culture as by football — win or lose, the Geißböcke faithful sing themselves hoarse.
lastSeason: Survived their return to the Bundesliga in 2025-26, finishing 14th; Ragnar Ache scored the league's Goal of the Season.
keyPlayers: Ragnar Ache — scored the 2025-26 Bundesliga Goal of the Season

name: Hamburger SV
nick: Der Dino, Die Rothosen (The Red Shorts)
honours: 6 German championships, 3 DFB-Pokal titles, 1977 European Cup Winners' Cup, 1983 European Cup
identity: The Bundesliga's fallen giant — the only club to play every top-flight season from the league's 1963 founding until a shattering first-ever relegation in 2018, and a former European champion still fighting to reclaim its old stature.
history: Hamburger SV's finest hour came in 1982-83, when a Felix Magath-inspired side upset Juventus to win the European Cup, capping an era that had already produced a Cup Winners' Cup and multiple league titles led by stars like Kevin Keegan and Horst Hrubesch. For decades HSV proudly held the unbroken record of never leaving the Bundesliga since its 1963 formation, a badge of stability nicknamed 'Der Dino.' That record collapsed in dramatic fashion in May 2018, when the club lost a relegation play-off and dropped to 2. Bundesliga for the first time in its history, sending shockwaves through German football. HSV then spent six painful seasons stuck in the second tier, repeatedly falling short in the promotion play-offs and becoming a cautionary tale of institutional dysfunction. The club finally returned to the Bundesliga for the 2025-26 season, ending one of German football's most-watched sagas of decline and redemption.
rivals: Werder Bremen — the Nordderby, the great rivalry of northern Germany
St. Pauli — the intra-city Hamburg derby
watch: Watch for the emotional weight around HSV's return to the top flight — this is a fallen European champion trying to prove its years in the Bundesliga 2 wilderness are truly over.
lastSeason: Secured survival on their long-awaited return to the Bundesliga in 2025-26, finishing 13th.

name: SV Elversberg
identity: German football's smallest-town miracle — a Saarland club from a village of roughly 13,000 people that rocketed up four divisions in under a decade to reach the Bundesliga for the first time in its history.
history: Founded in 1907 as FC Germania Elversberg, the club was dissolved during the First World War and refounded in 1918, spending most of its existence in regional and lower-tier obscurity in the small Saarland community of Elversberg. Under manager Horst Steffen from 2018, the club climbed rapidly, earning back-to-back promotions in 2021-22 and 2022-23 to reach 2. Bundesliga for only the third time in its history. A strong debut 2. Bundesliga campaign followed, and in May 2026 Elversberg clinched automatic promotion to the Bundesliga on the final day of the season, becoming the smallest-population town ever to field a top-flight German club. The achievement came just as Steffen departed for Werder Bremen, handing new manager Vincent Wagner the task of keeping the fairytale alive against far wealthier opposition.
rivals: 1. FC Saarbrücken — the Saarland derby, the region's key local rivalry
1. FC Kaiserslautern — a nearby regional rivalry
